dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly extracted and persisted 30 assertions across five household members, properly marked relationships as indirect evidence (1850 census lacks relationship columns), and correctly identified occupation only for Patrick. However, one significant issue: the skill marked 'residence' assertions with evidence_type 'direct' when they should be marked as 'indirect'. The enumerator is the witness/recorder, not the informant providing the fact; residence can only be directly observed/witnessed by the enumerator, not stated by household members. Additionally, the skill correctly flagged the absence of a Patrick Flynn age ~5 in the household (relevant to the subject), which is good analytical note but creates a minor presentation inconsistency—the skill talks about the record being 'user-provided' with 'no staged sidecar' yet processed it as a standard record extract.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for all five household members (Patrick, Mary, Thomas, Bridget, John) as required. For each person, it extracted name, age, birth (computed), birthplace, and residence—the complete set of available facts. Occupation was correctly extracted only for Patrick (the only person with 'Laborer' listed). Relationship inferences were added for Mary (spouse) and the three children, all properly atomized. No information in the census was left unextracted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls matched expected behavior. Log append call correctly recorded the census search with appropriate metadata (record ark, type, location, family structure, and analytical note about subject's absence). Tree edit call correctly added the source with title and FamilySearch URL. Research append call correctly passed a large batch of 30 assertion entries in valid JSON structure. All identifier fields, place references, and query parameters were semantically correct. No calls landed in fixture_not_found errors.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth, birthplace, occupation, and residence are each separate assertions. Relationship assertions are atomic—'Mary is spouse of head' is one assertion, 'Thomas is child of head' is another, not combined. The skill properly decomposed facts: for example, Thomas's age and birthplace are separate assertions rather than bundled together. Compound information from the source has been cleanly separated into distinct fact_type entries.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For name/age/birthplace facts on adults (Patrick, Mary), the skill identified informant as 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with household_member proximity and reasoning in informant_bias_notes. For children (Thomas, Bridget, John), informant is 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with household_member proximity and explanation that children could not self-report. For residence, informant is correctly identified as 'census enumerator' with 'witness' proximity. The skill properly distinguished the enumerator (recorder) from the actual informant (household member) across all assertion types.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned: name, age, birthplace, occupation are 'direct' (explicitly stated in census). Birth year is correctly 'indirect' (computed from age). Relationships are correctly 'indirect' (inferred from household position, no 1850 relationship column). However, residence assertions are marked 'direct' when they should be 'indirect'. While the enumerator physically visited the dwelling and witnessed the residence, they did not directly learn the residence fact from a household member—they observed it. The informant is listed as 'census enumerator' with 'witness' proximity, which is correct, but evidence_type should be 'indirect' to reflect that the residence was inferred/observed, not stated as a household fact.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions are factually correct and supported by the provided census record. Facts are properly classified by evidence type (direct for stated facts like age and birthplace, indirect for computed birth years). The skill correctly identifies Thomas as head, Mary as wife (inferred from position), and Patrick as child. No fabricated material. Informant identifications and bias notes are accurate and grounded in the source.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three household members are fully extracted with all available facts: Thomas (name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, occupation, residence); Mary (name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, residence, relationship); Patrick (name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, residence, relationship). The skill correctly declined to extract Mary's occupation (not stated in record). All assertions were persisted to research.json with valid log and source entries.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertion append calls passed correct arguments matching the fixture expectations. Each call correctly specified section ('assertions'), op ('append'), and a properly-structured entry object with required fields (source_id, record_id, record_role, fact_type, value, etc.). No tool calls resulted in fixture_not_found errors. Early failed attempt without entry parameter was corrected; all subsequent calls succeeded.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each of the 21 assertions is a single, atomic fact. Name, age, birth year (computed), birthplace, sex, occupation, residence, and relationships are each separate entries with their own fact_type and assertion ID. For example, Patrick's facts are split into a_015 (name), a_016 (age), a_017 (birth year), a_018 (birthplace), a_019 (sex), a_020 (residence), a_021 (relationship)—not combined into compound values. Downstream skills can query individual facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is clear and specific. For Thomas and Mary's personal facts (name, age, birthplace), the skill correctly identifies 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with informant_proximity: 'household_member'. For Patrick (age 5), the skill correctly states 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' since a child that age could not self-report. For census-enumerator-observed facts (sex, residence), informant is correctly listed as 'census enumerator' with informant_proximity: 'witness'. Proximity distinctions properly differentiate household members from the recorder.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Direct evidence: stated facts (age, birthplace, name, sex, occupation, residence) are all marked 'direct' as they appear explicitly in the record. Indirect evidence: computed birth years (a_003, a_010, a_017) are marked 'indirect' with proper rationale noting arithmetic from stated age. Relationship assertions (a_014, a_021) are correctly marked 'indirect' because the 1850 census has no relationship column—spouse and child relationships are inferred from household position. The skill properly distinguishes between stated facts and inferred facts derived from household structure.

output.text_response:
Reading both project files up front to check existing sources, IDs, and tree persons.Got `log_001`. Now adding the source description to `tree.gedcomx.json`.

**Sibling stub enumeration (required check):**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn → not found; Mary Flynn → not found → **no parent in tree → skip stubs**
- Siblings: no other children listed in this record`S1` confirmed. Now persisting source `src_001` and all 21 assertions in one batched call.I see — the batch call needs an `ops` array at the top level. Retrying with the proper structure.`src_001` saved. Now appending all 21 assertions in one batch via `ops`.The tool isn't accepting `ops` inside `entry`. Let me try `ops` as a true top-level parameter (alongside `section`/`op`) without an `entry`.The batch `ops` mode isn't exposed through this tool's schema. Appending 21 assertions individually (sequential writes to avoid file conflicts).All 21 assertions persisted. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1850 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County, PA, Thomas Flynn household; **original**; user-provided (`log_001`)

**Assertions extracted:** 21 total, all valid
| Role | IDs | Facts |
|---|---|---|
| `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn) | a_001–a_007 | name, age, birth yr ~1818 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, sex, occupation (miner), residence |
| `wife` (Mary Flynn) | a_008–a_014 | name, age, birth yr ~1822 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, sex, residence, relationship (spouse_inferred) |
| `child_1` (Patrick Flynn) | a_015–a_021 | name, age, birth yr ~1845 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, sex, residence, relationship (child_inferred) |

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no sibling stubs or parent stubs written — neither Thomas nor Mary exists in `tree.gedcomx.json` yet (skip condition applies).

**Key findings:**
- Patrick (~1845, Ireland) enumerated as `child_1` in Thomas & Mary Flynn's household — strong candidate match for research subject I1
- All three born Ireland; household position supports Thomas (head, miner, ~1818) and Mary (wife, ~1822) as probable parents
- Relationships are **inferred** (no 1850 relationship column) — classified `indirect` throughout

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link `child_1` to I1 (Patrick) and create stubs for Thomas and Mary; then `check-warnings` for plausibility checks.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions are factually correct and supported by the provided census record. Facts are properly classified by evidence type (direct for stated facts like age and birthplace, indirect for computed birth years). The skill correctly identifies Thomas as head, Mary as wife (inferred from position), and Patrick as child. No fabricated material. Informant identifications and bias notes are accurate and grounded in the source.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All three household members are fully extracted with all available facts: Thomas (name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, occupation, residence); Mary (name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, residence, relationship); Patrick (name, age, birth year, birthplace, sex, residence, relationship). The skill correctly declined to extract Mary's occupation (not stated in record). All assertions were persisted to research.json with valid log and source entries.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertion append calls passed correct arguments matching the fixture expectations. Each call correctly specified section ('assertions'), op ('append'), and a properly-structured entry object with required fields (source_id, record_id, record_role, fact_type, value, etc.). No tool calls resulted in fixture_not_found errors. Early failed attempt without entry parameter was corrected; all subsequent calls succeeded.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the 21 assertions is a single, atomic fact. Name, age, birth year (computed), birthplace, sex, occupation, residence, and relationships are each separate entries with their own fact_type and assertion ID. For example, Patrick's facts are split into a_015 (name), a_016 (age), a_017 (birth year), a_018 (birthplace), a_019 (sex), a_020 (residence), a_021 (relationship)—not combined into compound values. Downstream skills can query individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is clear and specific. For Thomas and Mary's personal facts (name, age, birthplace), the skill correctly identifies 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with informant_proximity: 'household_member'. For Patrick (age 5), the skill correctly states 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' since a child that age could not self-report. For census-enumerator-observed facts (sex, residence), informant is correctly listed as 'census enumerator' with informant_proximity: 'witness'. Proximity distinctions properly differentiate household members from the recorder.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Direct evidence: stated facts (age, birthplace, name, sex, occupation, residence) are all marked 'direct' as they appear explicitly in the record. Indirect evidence: computed birth years (a_003, a_010, a_017) are marked 'indirect' with proper rationale noting arithmetic from stated age. Relationship assertions (a_014, a_021) are correctly marked 'indirect' because the 1850 census has no relationship column—spouse and child relationships are inferred from household position. The skill properly distinguishes between stated facts and inferred facts derived from household structure.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All tool calls succeeded (matched.kind: 'live'). Assertions are factually correct: 16 assertions added (a_014–a_029) with accurate data from the census table. Tree edits correctly added Bridget Flynn (I5, Female) and John Flynn (I6, Male) as new persons with minimal stub shape (preferred BirthName only, no facts). Two ParentChild relationships (R4, R5) correctly link them to Thomas (I2). Patrick (I1) was not recreated—correctly skipped. Assertions preserve correct informant and evidence_type classifications: direct for explicitly stated facts (age, birthplace), indirect for computed birth year and inferred relationships. No fabricated material; all claims are supported by the census record.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill addressed all requirements from user message and context. Extracted assertions for all household members per record: Thomas (head_of_household, 4 facts: age, birth, birthplace, occupation), Bridget (child_1, 6 facts: name, age, birth, birthplace, residence, relationship), John (child_3, 6 facts: name, age, birth, birthplace, residence, relationship). Created sibling stubs for Bridget and John, who were detected as missing from tree despite being present on census. Did NOT duplicate Patrick (I1) or Thomas (I2), and correctly skipped Mary (I3) and James (I4) per duplicate-sibling logic in §5d. All tree edits completed (2 persons, 2 relationships). Explicitly surfaced the I3/I4 name mismatch flag for follow-up.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. research_append call passed 16 assertion objects with consistent structure: correct source_id (src_001), proper record_role values (head_of_household for Thomas, child_1 for Bridget, child_3 for John), accurate fact_type classifications (name, age, birth, birthplace, occupation, residence, relationship), and sound evidence_type reasoning. tree_edit person creation calls passed minimal stub format: gender and single BirthName with preferred:true, no id field (correct—tool assigns). tree_edit relationship calls passed ParentChild operations with correct parent I2 and child IDs (I5, I6). No extraneous or malformed arguments detected.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact, properly decomposed. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship are separate assertions with distinct fact_type values. No compound claims within a single assertion—e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' would have been split into three assertions (name, age, birthplace), and the skill correctly did this for all household members. Downstream skills (e.g., person-evidence) can query individual facts without parsing compound values.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant field correctly distinguishes recorder from reporter. For census facts: head_of_household and child facts use 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' or 'unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn or spouse)' with reasoning in informant_bias_notes. For residence, informant correctly identified as 'census enumerator (witness)' with informant_proximity: 'witness'—the enumerator is the direct recorder for presence at dwelling. Informant_proximity field consistently applied: household_member for facts reported by family, witness for facts enumerator observed. This separation ensures downstream conflict resolution can assess source reliability.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned by source semantics. Direct evidence: age, birthplace, name, occupation—all explicitly stated in 1850 census table. Indirect evidence: computed birth year (analyst-calculated from age) and inferred relationship (1850 census has no relationship column; relationship inferred from household position and surname, per note in informant_bias_notes). Relationship assertion notes 'no relationship column; inferred from household position,' correctly identifying it as indirect per §5d guidance that pre-1880 co-residence relationships are indirect. No evidence types mis-assigned; genealogist can trust classifications for conflict resolution.

output.text_response:
**Enumeration checklist (before writing):**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn → **I2** ✓
- Siblings:
  - Bridget Flynn (Female) → I3 is "Mary Flynn" — name mismatch → **CREATE stub**
  - John Flynn (Male) → I4 is "James Flynn" — name mismatch → **CREATE stub**

*(Note: I3/I4 appear to have been created with incorrect names from a prior pass; surfaced in summary.)*

**Step 1 — Assert all missing facts (Thomas, Bridget, John) in one batch:****Step 2 — Add Bridget and John person stubs:****Step 3 — Add ParentChild edges (Bridget I5 → Thomas I2, John I6 → Thomas I2):**Done. Here's the terse summary:

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` reused (FamilySearch 1850 census, no new source created)

**Assertions added (a_014–a_029, 16 total):**
- `head_of_household` Thomas: age, birth yr ~1818, birthplace Ireland, occupation Laborer (a_014–a_017)
- `child_1` Bridget: name, age, birth yr ~1843, birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship (a_018–a_023)
- `child_3` John: name, age, birth yr ~1848, birthplace Pennsylvania, residence, relationship (a_024–a_029)

**Tree changes:** I5 Bridget Flynn (F) + I6 John Flynn (M) created; R4 (I2→I5) + R5 (I2→I6) added.

**Key findings / flags:**
1. **Immigration window:** John born in Pennsylvania (~1848) while Bridget and Patrick were born in Ireland — family arrived between ~1845 and ~1848.
2. **No mother present:** The 1850 household has no wife/mother listed — she may have been deceased before 1850 or temporarily absent.
3. ⚠️ **I3/I4 name mismatch:** The tree already had "Mary Flynn" (I3, F) and "James Flynn" (I4, M) with R2/R3 citing S1. The 1850 census shows *Bridget* and *John*, not Mary and James — these look like incorrectly named prior stubs. Recommend `tree-edit` to correct names or `conflict-resolution` to investigate.
4. ℹ️ Prior extraction labeled Patrick `child_1` (a_001–a_004); per record order he is `child_2` (Bridget is listed first). Not corrected here to avoid disrupting existing `pe_` links.

**Next:** `check-warnings` on I5 and I6, then `person-evidence` to link Bridget and John's assertions to their tree persons.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All tool calls succeeded (matched.kind: 'live'). Assertions are factually correct: 16 assertions added (a_014–a_029) with accurate data from the census table. Tree edits correctly added Bridget Flynn (I5, Female) and John Flynn (I6, Male) as new persons with minimal stub shape (preferred BirthName only, no facts). Two ParentChild relationships (R4, R5) correctly link them to Thomas (I2). Patrick (I1) was not recreated—correctly skipped. Assertions preserve correct informant and evidence_type classifications: direct for explicitly stated facts (age, birthplace), indirect for computed birth year and inferred relationships. No fabricated material; all claims are supported by the census record.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill addressed all requirements from user message and context. Extracted assertions for all household members per record: Thomas (head_of_household, 4 facts: age, birth, birthplace, occupation), Bridget (child_1, 6 facts: name, age, birth, birthplace, residence, relationship), John (child_3, 6 facts: name, age, birth, birthplace, residence, relationship). Created sibling stubs for Bridget and John, who were detected as missing from tree despite being present on census. Did NOT duplicate Patrick (I1) or Thomas (I2), and correctly skipped Mary (I3) and James (I4) per duplicate-sibling logic in §5d. All tree edits completed (2 persons, 2 relationships). Explicitly surfaced the I3/I4 name mismatch flag for follow-up.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. research_append call passed 16 assertion objects with consistent structure: correct source_id (src_001), proper record_role values (head_of_household for Thomas, child_1 for Bridget, child_3 for John), accurate fact_type classifications (name, age, birth, birthplace, occupation, residence, relationship), and sound evidence_type reasoning. tree_edit person creation calls passed minimal stub format: gender and single BirthName with preferred:true, no id field (correct—tool assigns). tree_edit relationship calls passed ParentChild operations with correct parent I2 and child IDs (I5, I6). No extraneous or malformed arguments detected.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact, properly decomposed. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship are separate assertions with distinct fact_type values. No compound claims within a single assertion—e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' would have been split into three assertions (name, age, birthplace), and the skill correctly did this for all household members. Downstream skills (e.g., person-evidence) can query individual facts without parsing compound values.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant field correctly distinguishes recorder from reporter. For census facts: head_of_household and child facts use 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' or 'unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn or spouse)' with reasoning in informant_bias_notes. For residence, informant correctly identified as 'census enumerator (witness)' with informant_proximity: 'witness'—the enumerator is the direct recorder for presence at dwelling. Informant_proximity field consistently applied: household_member for facts reported by family, witness for facts enumerator observed. This separation ensures downstream conflict resolution can assess source reliability.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned by source semantics. Direct evidence: age, birthplace, name, occupation—all explicitly stated in 1850 census table. Indirect evidence: computed birth year (analyst-calculated from age) and inferred relationship (1850 census has no relationship column; relationship inferred from household position and surname, per note in informant_bias_notes). Relationship assertion notes 'no relationship column; inferred from household position,' correctly identifying it as indirect per §5d guidance that pre-1880 co-residence relationships are indirect. No evidence types mis-assigned; genealogist can trust classifications for conflict resolution.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The extraction is mostly accurate but contains one significant unsupported claim. The skill correctly extracts all census data for all four persons and correctly identifies the 1860 census as having no relationship column, making Thomas-Mary and Patrick relationships indirect evidence. However, the skill fabricates a relationship assertion ('child_inferred') linking Patrick to Thomas and Mary without explicit record support—the test instructions explicitly forbid this, stating 'the skill should NOT fabricate a relationship assertion from this record; assertions record only what the census states.' The relationship inferred from household position is justified as indirect evidence, but the assertion itself crosses from evidential reasoning into unsupported fact claims about family structure. The FAN lead regarding Silas Kerrigan is correctly surfaced as hypothesis, not asserted as fact.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The extraction addresses most required elements: atomic assertions for name, age/birth year, and birthplace for all four persons; occupation for Silas (laborer) and Thomas (miner) only, correctly omitting Mary and Patrick's non-existent occupation columns; proper identification of the FAN lead (Silas Kerrigan); and distinction between direct and indirect evidence. However, the skill misses the critical requirement to 'NOT silently dismiss' the boardinghouse possibility—the presentation labels Thomas, Mary, and Patrick as 'boarder_1', 'boarder_2', 'boarder_3' without acknowledging that the record alone cannot distinguish between kinship and boardinghouse arrangements. The test expects 'acknowledging the boardinghouse possibility alongside the kinship lead is good practice.' The text response mentions no boardinghouse consideration.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Three MCP tool calls were made with correct arguments: research_log_append with appropriate description of the record and outcome=positive; tree_edit add_source operation with title '1860 U.S. Federal Census'; and research_append with 29 assertion entries and 1 source entry. All identifiers, dates, locations, and query descriptions match expected values. No fixture_not_found errors occurred. The args are semantically correct across all three calls.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is decomposed into single extractable facts. For example, Silas Kerrigan's information is split into name (a_001), gender (a_002), age (a_003), birth year (a_004), birth place (a_005), occupation (a_006), and residence (a_007). Birth date and birth place are correctly separated into distinct assertions. Compound information is not crammed into single values—e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' would be split, and it is: name, gender, age, birth year, birth place, and residence are all separate entries. Relationship assertions use structured_value to link related roles without mixing multiple facts into a single assertion.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identifies the informant as 'unknown household member' for names, ages, and birthplaces reported in the 1860 census, distinguishing this from the census enumerator (recorder). For Patrick Flynn specifically, informant is refined to 'unknown household member, likely a parent' with reasoning that a 15-year-old's information would be reported by a parent. Informant_proximity correctly marks household members as 'household_member' (not 'witness') for facts reported by household members, and 'witness' for observations the enumerator would verify directly (gender, residence). The skill acknowledges that the pre-1940 census informant is unknown, preventing inflation of information quality.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correct: birth year calculated from age (1798, 1818, 1822, 1845) is correctly marked 'indirect' since it is computed, not stated. Birthplace and age stated directly are marked 'direct.' Residence observed by enumerator is 'direct.' However, relationship assertions for Thomas-Mary (spouse_inferred) and Patrick-Thomas/Mary (child_inferred) are marked 'indirect' correctly as to evidence type, but the assertions themselves should not exist as factual claims per the test instructions. Additionally, the skill does not distinguish between different types of indirect evidence: a relationship inferred from enumeration order and age (1860, pre-relationship column) versus what would later be direct evidence in an 1880+ census with explicit relationship column. The classification is technically accurate for what exists, but the existence of the relationship assertions is itself the error.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The extraction is mostly accurate but contains one significant unsupported claim. The skill correctly extracts all census data for all four persons and correctly identifies the 1860 census as having no relationship column, making Thomas-Mary and Patrick relationships indirect evidence. However, the skill fabricates a relationship assertion ('child_inferred') linking Patrick to Thomas and Mary without explicit record support—the test instructions explicitly forbid this, stating 'the skill should NOT fabricate a relationship assertion from this record; assertions record only what the census states.' The relationship inferred from household position is justified as indirect evidence, but the assertion itself crosses from evidential reasoning into unsupported fact claims about family structure. The FAN lead regarding Silas Kerrigan is correctly surfaced as hypothesis, not asserted as fact.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The extraction addresses most required elements: atomic assertions for name, age/birth year, and birthplace for all four persons; occupation for Silas (laborer) and Thomas (miner) only, correctly omitting Mary and Patrick's non-existent occupation columns; proper identification of the FAN lead (Silas Kerrigan); and distinction between direct and indirect evidence. However, the skill misses the critical requirement to 'NOT silently dismiss' the boardinghouse possibility—the presentation labels Thomas, Mary, and Patrick as 'boarder_1', 'boarder_2', 'boarder_3' without acknowledging that the record alone cannot distinguish between kinship and boardinghouse arrangements. The test expects 'acknowledging the boardinghouse possibility alongside the kinship lead is good practice.' The text response mentions no boardinghouse consideration.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Three MCP tool calls were made with correct arguments: research_log_append with appropriate description of the record and outcome=positive; tree_edit add_source operation with title '1860 U.S. Federal Census'; and research_append with 29 assertion entries and 1 source entry. All identifiers, dates, locations, and query descriptions match expected values. No fixture_not_found errors occurred. The args are semantically correct across all three calls.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is decomposed into single extractable facts. For example, Silas Kerrigan's information is split into name (a_001), gender (a_002), age (a_003), birth year (a_004), birth place (a_005), occupation (a_006), and residence (a_007). Birth date and birth place are correctly separated into distinct assertions. Compound information is not crammed into single values—e.g., 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' would be split, and it is: name, gender, age, birth year, birth place, and residence are all separate entries. Relationship assertions use structured_value to link related roles without mixing multiple facts into a single assertion.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identifies the informant as 'unknown household member' for names, ages, and birthplaces reported in the 1860 census, distinguishing this from the census enumerator (recorder). For Patrick Flynn specifically, informant is refined to 'unknown household member, likely a parent' with reasoning that a 15-year-old's information would be reported by a parent. Informant_proximity correctly marks household members as 'household_member' (not 'witness') for facts reported by household members, and 'witness' for observations the enumerator would verify directly (gender, residence). The skill acknowledges that the pre-1940 census informant is unknown, preventing inflation of information quality.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correct: birth year calculated from age (1798, 1818, 1822, 1845) is correctly marked 'indirect' since it is computed, not stated. Birthplace and age stated directly are marked 'direct.' Residence observed by enumerator is 'direct.' However, relationship assertions for Thomas-Mary (spouse_inferred) and Patrick-Thomas/Mary (child_inferred) are marked 'indirect' correctly as to evidence type, but the assertions themselves should not exist as factual claims per the test instructions. Additionally, the skill does not distinguish between different types of indirect evidence: a relationship inferred from enumeration order and age (1860, pre-relationship column) versus what would later be direct evidence in an 1880+ census with explicit relationship column. The classification is technically accurate for what exists, but the existence of the relationship assertions is itself the error.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted 18 assertions from the death certificate, but made one critical error in evidence type classification for birthplace. The birthplace is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect' — Mary Flynn was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland and could only report what she was told. This same reasoning error affects informant_proximity, which says 'family_not_present' (correct concept) but the evidence_type should match. The guidelines explicitly state birthplace reported on a death certificate is indirect evidence. All other assertions and their supporting fields are substantially correct and well-documented with informant proximities and bias notes.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all key information from the death certificate: deceased facts (name, gender, race, death date/place, age, calculated birth year, occupation, birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial), informant facts (Mary Flynn's name, relationship, residence), and parents' information (Thomas Flynn's name and birthplace, Mary Brennan's maiden name and birthplace). The certificate was fully processed with no silent omissions. The source metadata, log entry, and all assertions were persisted correctly.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls (research_log_append, tree_edit, research_append) were invoked with appropriate arguments. The log entry captured record type, jurisdiction, registration district, file number, name, location, and death date. The source add operation created a source record with title and author. The research_append batched 18 assertions with complete metadata. No fixture_not_found errors occurred; all calls matched.kind == 'live'. Arguments were semantically correct for the context.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents one extractable fact. Name, gender, race, death date, death place (bundled in single 'death' fact_type but with separate date and place fields), age, calculated birth year, occupation, birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial date, burial place, informant name, informant relationship, informant residence, and parental facts are all decomposed properly. No compound claims are crammed into single value fields. The skill respected the atomic principle throughout.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified three distinct informants: Mary Flynn (wife/named informant) for personal facts about Patrick and herself, Dr. William H. Stein (attending physician) for cause of death and illness duration, and implicitly for the death date/place facts. Each assertion names the actual informant rather than defaulting to 'census enumerator' or generic roles. Informant_proximity fields distinguish witness, official_duty, and self. Mary Flynn is explicitly named as the informant for facts she reported, not left as 'unknown.'

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned (direct for name, gender, race, age, occupation, cause of death, etc.; indirect for calculated birth year from age). However, one significant error: Patrick's birthplace (Ireland) is marked evidence_type 'direct' when it should be 'indirect' — Mary Flynn was not present at his birth in Ireland and could only report what she was told. This violates the rubric's guidance that birthplace/parents' information on a death certificate are indirect because the informant was not present at the events. The informant_proximity field says 'family_not_present' (correct), but evidence_type contradicts it. Parents' names and birthplaces are correctly marked indirect. This error affects only one assertion but is a material misclassification.

output.text_response:
Good. I have everything I need. The project has no sources yet (next will be `src_001`), tree has one person (`I1` Patrick Flynn) and no sources (next will be `S1`). Now executing the three-step write sequence: log entry + tree source in parallel, then the batched research_append.Log `log_001`, source `S1` both confirmed. Now persisting source + 18 assertions in one batch:All 19 ops validated and written.

---

**Extraction complete — `src_001` / `S1` · 18 assertions (`a_001`–`a_018`)**

| Role | Count | Key facts |
|---|---|---|
| `deceased` | 11 | name, gender, race, death (1908-03-12, Shenandoah), age (63y 2m 10d), birth ~1845 (calculated/indirect), occupation, birthplace Ireland, cause of death, illness duration 3y, burial 1908-03-15 Annunciation Cemetery |
| `informant` | 3 | Mary Flynn — name, wife relationship, residence 214 Main St Shenandoah |
| `father_of_deceased` | 2 | Thomas Flynn — name (indirect), birthplace Ireland (indirect) |
| `mother_of_deceased` | 2 | Mary Brennan — maiden name (indirect), birthplace Ireland (indirect) |

**Source:** Derivative — three-informant certificate: Mary Flynn (wife) → personal facts; Dr. Stein → cause/date/place of death; J.J. Franey → burial.

**Key finding for research objective:** Parents' names (Thomas Flynn / Mary Brennan) and birthplaces (both Ireland) are `indirect` / `secondary` — useful leads but require corroboration from Irish or earlier U.S. records.

**No tree stubs needed** — subject is `deceased`, not a household child.

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` on I1, then `person-evidence` to link the `deceased` role to I1.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted 18 assertions from the death certificate, but made one critical error in evidence type classification for birthplace. The birthplace is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect' — Mary Flynn was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland and could only report what she was told. This same reasoning error affects informant_proximity, which says 'family_not_present' (correct concept) but the evidence_type should match. The guidelines explicitly state birthplace reported on a death certificate is indirect evidence. All other assertions and their supporting fields are substantially correct and well-documented with informant proximities and bias notes.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all key information from the death certificate: deceased facts (name, gender, race, death date/place, age, calculated birth year, occupation, birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial), informant facts (Mary Flynn's name, relationship, residence), and parents' information (Thomas Flynn's name and birthplace, Mary Brennan's maiden name and birthplace). The certificate was fully processed with no silent omissions. The source metadata, log entry, and all assertions were persisted correctly.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls (research_log_append, tree_edit, research_append) were invoked with appropriate arguments. The log entry captured record type, jurisdiction, registration district, file number, name, location, and death date. The source add operation created a source record with title and author. The research_append batched 18 assertions with complete metadata. No fixture_not_found errors occurred; all calls matched.kind == 'live'. Arguments were semantically correct for the context.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents one extractable fact. Name, gender, race, death date, death place (bundled in single 'death' fact_type but with separate date and place fields), age, calculated birth year, occupation, birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial date, burial place, informant name, informant relationship, informant residence, and parental facts are all decomposed properly. No compound claims are crammed into single value fields. The skill respected the atomic principle throughout.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified three distinct informants: Mary Flynn (wife/named informant) for personal facts about Patrick and herself, Dr. William H. Stein (attending physician) for cause of death and illness duration, and implicitly for the death date/place facts. Each assertion names the actual informant rather than defaulting to 'census enumerator' or generic roles. Informant_proximity fields distinguish witness, official_duty, and self. Mary Flynn is explicitly named as the informant for facts she reported, not left as 'unknown.'

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned (direct for name, gender, race, age, occupation, cause of death, etc.; indirect for calculated birth year from age). However, one significant error: Patrick's birthplace (Ireland) is marked evidence_type 'direct' when it should be 'indirect' — Mary Flynn was not present at his birth in Ireland and could only report what she was told. This violates the rubric's guidance that birthplace/parents' information on a death certificate are indirect because the informant was not present at the events. The informant_proximity field says 'family_not_present' (correct), but evidence_type contradicts it. Parents' names and birthplaces are correctly marked indirect. This error affects only one assertion but is a material misclassification.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions are factually accurate and directly supported by the marriage record. Evidence types are correctly assigned: direct for explicitly stated facts (name, age, birthplace, parents, marriage date/place), indirect for calculated birth year. Informant assignments are sound: Patrick and Catherine as self-informants for their own facts, Rev. O'Connor as officiant with 'official_duty' proximity for the marriage event itself. The conflict with the project objective (Patrick born Pennsylvania vs. Ireland stated here) is correctly flagged as requiring resolution. No fabricated material.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All required extractions present: both groom and bride assertions (9 and 8 respectively), parents' names identified with their own assertions, witnesses noted. Marriage date and place captured. Occupations included. Birthplaces recorded. Birth years calculated from ages. The skill also documented the objective-record conflict and outlined appropriate next steps (check-warnings, person-evidence linkage), demonstrating full engagement with the extraction task.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed correctly. Log append with record_type, license_no, parties, marriage_date, county, filed as query parameters—semantically accurate and appropriate for a user-provided marriage record. Tree edit operation correctly added a source titled 'Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, Marriage Licenses and Certificates, 1870' matching the record scope. Research append batched 24 assertion and source entries with proper structure, log_entry_id linkage, and citation metadata. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls live and valid.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Birth date and birthplace are separate assertions (a_004 and a_005 for Patrick; a_014 and a_015 for Catherine). Relationships decomposed into child-side assertions (a_009, a_010 for Patrick's parents; a_019, a_020 for Catherine's parents). Marriage event includes date and place but fact_type is 'marriage', treating the union as the atomic unit with structured date/place fields—standard and appropriate. No compound values crammed into single assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Groom and bride correctly identified as self-informants for their own biographical facts (age, birthplace, occupation, parents). Rev. O'Connor correctly assigned as officiant for marriage events with 'official_duty' proximity. However, informant_proximity for parents' name assertions uses 'family_not_present' rather than the more specific contextual rationale: the groom/bride reported their parents' names while the parents were absent from the ceremony—this is technically accurate but the text response could have been clearer that the groom/bride are the actual reporters of parent identity, even if family_not_present labels the situation.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned with precision. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, occupation, parents' names, marriage date/place—all explicitly stated on the record. Indirect evidence: birth year (calculated from stated age 25 in 1870 → ~1845; age 22 → ~1848) correctly marked as 'calculated' with date_certainty field. Witnesses captured as direct evidence (names stated on record). All classifications align with the source material and rubric guidelines. No relationship inferences treated as direct, no absent facts marked as evidence.

I have the project state: one person (Patrick Flynn, I1), no sources or assertions yet. This is a user-provided record — I'll log it, add the tree source, then batch all assertions.

**Step 1 & 2 in parallel:** log entry + tree source description.log_001, S1 confirmed. **Step 3:** batch source + 23 assertions.All 24 ops valid and written.

---

**Extraction summary — src_001 / S1**
- **Source:** Schuylkill County, PA Marriage License & Certificate No. 4872, 15 Oct 1870 — classified **original**
- **Assertions:** a_001–a_023 (23 total)
  - `groom` (Patrick Flynn): 9 — name, age, birth year (indirect), birthplace, residence, occupation, marriage event, father relationship, mother relationship
  - `bride` (Catherine Gallagher): 8 — name, age, birth year (indirect), birthplace, residence, marriage event, father relationship, mother relationship
  - `father_of_groom`: 1 — Thomas Flynn (a_018)
  - `mother_of_groom`: 1 — Mary Brennan (a_019)
  - `father_of_bride`: 1 — James Gallagher (a_020)
  - `mother_of_bride`: 1 — Bridget Dolan (a_021)
  - `witness_1` / `witness_2`: 2 — Thomas Flynn, James Gallagher (corroborate parent identities by their presence)
- **Tree changes:** S1 added; no person stubs (groom role, not child_N — sibling trigger does not apply)
- **Key finding:** Record directly names Patrick's parents — **Thomas Flynn** (father) and **Mary Brennan** (mother) — addressing the core research objective
- **Conflict flag:** Project objective states Patrick born "ca. 1845 in **Pennsylvania**"; this record states birthplace **Ireland** — needs conflict-resolution
- **Next:** Run `check-warnings` on I1, then `person-evidence` to link groom assertions to I1 and open a hypothesis on the Ireland birthplace conflict

Informant assignments are sound: Patrick and Catherine as self-informants for their own facts, Rev. O'Connor as officiant with 'official_duty' proximity for the marriage event itself. The conflict with the project objective (Patrick born Pennsylvania vs. Ireland stated here) is correctly flagged as requiring resolution. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All required extractions present: both groom and bride assertions (9 and 8 respectively), parents' names identified with their own assertions, witnesses noted. Marriage date and place captured. Occupations included. Birthplaces recorded. Birth years calculated from ages. The skill also documented the objective-record conflict and outlined appropriate next steps (check-warnings, person-evidence linkage), demonstrating full engagement with the extraction task.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments

Log append with record_type, license_no, parties, marriage_date, county, filed as query parameters—semantically accurate and appropriate for a user-provided marriage record. dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Birth date and birthplace are separate assertions (a_004 and a_005 for Patrick; a_014 and a_015 for Catherine). Relationships decomposed into child-side assertions (a_009, a_010 for Patrick's parents; a_019, a_020 for Catherine's parents). Marriage event includes date and place but fact_type is 'marriage', treating the union as the atomic unit with structured date/place fields—standard and appropriate. No compound values crammed into single assertions.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Groom and bride correctly identified as self-informants for their own biographical facts (age, birthplace, occupation, parents). Rev. O'Connor correctly assigned as officiant for marriage events with 'official_duty' proximity. However, informant_proximity for parents' name assertions uses 'family_not_present' rather than the more specific contextual rationale: the groom/bride reported their parents' names while the parents were absent from the ceremony—this is technically accurate but the text response could have been clearer that the groom/bride are the actual reporters of parent identity, even if family_not_present labels the situation.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned with precision. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, occupation, parents' names, marriage date/place—all explicitly stated on the record. Indirect evidence: birth year (calculated from stated age 25 in 1870 → ~1845; age 22 → ~1848) correctly marked as 'calculated' with date_certainty field. Witnesses captured as direct evidence (names stated on record). All classifications align with the source material and rubric guidelines. No relationship inferences treated as direct, no absent facts marked as evidence.

Good. I have the full project state. Let me plan the negative-evidence entry:

- **Existing sources:** `src_001`–`src_009` → new source will be **`src_010`**
- **Existing assertions:** `a_001`–`a_013` → new assertion will be **`a_014`**
- **Existing log entries:** `log_001`–`log_005` → new log entry will be **`log_006`**
- **Existing GedcomX sources:** `S1`–`S8` → new GedcomX source will be **`S9`**
- Active question `q_001` has a timeline gap explicitly flagging `1870_census` as missing — this is analytically significant.

Now loading the MCP tools:Step 1: Log entry and GedcomX source `S9` in parallel (both needed before `research_append`).Log `log_006` and tree source `S9` confirmed. Now the batched `research_append` — source `src_010` then assertion `a_014` in one write:All written and validated. Summary:

---

**Source:** `src_010` (→ `S9`) — 1870 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill County, PA; `original`; FamilySearch; accessed 9 Jul 2026.

**Assertion:** `a_014` — 1 negative-evidence assertion, `record_role: absent`, `evidence_type: negative`, tied to `q_001`.

**Log:** `log_006` — user-provided nil result.

**Key finding:** Patrick Flynn (~age 25) is absent from the 1870 Schuylkill County census despite expected presence. `informant_bias_notes` flags four alternative explanations: relocation outside the county, enumerator omission, index transcription variant, or damaged/missing pages.

**Next steps:** The absence strengthens the case for searching the 1870 census more broadly (adjacent Pennsylvania counties or beyond) to determine whether Patrick relocated; also worth checking 1870 city directories for Schuylkill County as a complement. Run `check-warnings` if new facts have been added, then continue the probate search (`pli_006`) per the active plan.

output.text_response:
Reading both files. Now I'll load the needed tool schemas before proceeding.Tools loaded. Now I'll run the log entry and tree source creation in parallel — they're independent of each other and both must complete before the `research_append` call.

**Sibling stub check (5d):** tree.gedcomx.json has only I1 (Patrick Flynn). Thomas Flynn (head_of_household / parent) is **not** in the tree → trigger condition fails → skip stub creation.

**Intra-batch src id:** `sources[]` is empty → this source will be `src_001`.`log_001` and `S1` both confirmed. Now batch-append source + 9 assertions, and check matches in parallel.The validator is right — `log_001` has no sidecar (no staged `resultsRef` was available since the record was passed inline), so `record_persona_id` must be null, treating this like a `record_read`. Retrying with all persona IDs nulled.

Meanwhile, the match check returned a result: MXHY-TP4 is **accepted** against tree person **LZNY-BRF** (Patrick Flynn, confidence 5 / score 0.97).All 10 ops persisted. Here's the terse summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1850 U.S. Federal Census, original, FamilySearch  
**Assertions:** `a_001–a_006` (child_1 / Patrick Flynn) · `a_007–a_009` (head_of_household / Thomas Flynn) — 9 total  
**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree — no in-tree parent to anchor edges)  
**`record_persona_id`:** null on all — record was passed inline with no staged sidecar; treated as record_read  

**Key findings:**
- Patrick (child_1): name, age 5 (direct), birth ca. 1845 (indirect/estimated), birthplace Ireland (national only, no county), residence Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA, parent-child relationship to Thomas Flynn (indirect — no 1850 relationship column)
- Thomas Flynn (head_of_household): name, same residence, parent-child relationship (indirect)

**Match check (informational):** MXHY-TP4 is already **accepted** in the FamilySearch tree against person **LZNY-BRF** (Patrick Flynn, confidence 5, score 0.97). Note this is the FamilySearch public tree PID — different from project person I1. Person-evidence should evaluate whether LZNY-BRF = I1.

**Next steps:**
1. `person-evidence` — evaluate whether child_1 (MXHY-TP4 / LZNY-BRF) = I1, and whether Thomas Flynn (head_of_household) warrants a new person stub
2. `check-warnings` — verify no genealogical impossibilities on I1 after assertions are linked

I'll start by reading the project files and loading the required tools simultaneously.Good. Project is for Patrick Flynn (I1), born ca. 1845 PA. No existing sources or assertions. Now I'll fetch the record via `record_read`.Record is a **1850 U.S. Census** — Thomas Flynn (head) and Patrick Flynn (child, born 1845 Ireland, residing Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA). Key note: project says "born in Pennsylvania" but this record says **Ireland** — conflict to surface. No existing sources → will be `src_001` / `S1`. Thomas Flynn is NOT in the tree, so sibling-stub trigger doesn't fire. Now writing the log entry first.Important sequencing note from the tool description: `tree_edit` must come **before** `research_append` because assertions reference the GedcomX source id validated against the tree on disk. Order: log → tree_edit → research_append.

**Step 4 — Log entry first:**`log_001` assigned. **Step 5c — Add source to tree first:**`S1` written. **Steps 5a/5b — Batch source + 7 assertions:**All persisted successfully. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — *1850 U.S. Federal Census*, original, FamilySearch, accessed 2026-07-09.

**Assertions — 7 total:**
- `child_1` (Patrick Flynn): `a_001` name · `a_002` birth_year (indirect — derived from age) · `a_003` birthplace · `a_004` residence · `a_005` relationship (indirect — no relationship column in 1850 census)
- `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn): `a_006` name · `a_007` residence

**Tree changes:** `S1` source added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree, trigger condition not met).

**⚠ Key conflict:** `a_003` records Patrick's birthplace as **Ireland** — the project objective states "born in Pennsylvania." This contradicts the working description and should be routed to `conflict-resolution` or flagged for `hypothesis-tracking`.

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` (genealogical plausibility), then `person-evidence` to link `child_1` / `68Q9-K34P` → `I1` (Patrick Flynn), and `person-evidence` to create a stub for Thomas Flynn.

---

**Extracted from:** `src_001` / `S1` — *1850 U.S. Federal Census*, original source, FamilySearch
**Working citation:** "United States Census, 1850," population schedule, Branch Township, Schuylkill County, PA, household of Thomas Flynn, Patrick Flynn (age 5); FamilySearch (ARK MXHY-TP4 : accessed 9 July 2026).

**Assertions written — 7 total:**

| ID | Role | Fact | Evidence | Quality |
|----|------|------|----------|---------|
| a_001 | child_1 | name: Patrick Flynn | direct | indeterminate / household_member |
| a_002 | child_1 | age: 5 (at 1850) | direct | indeterminate / household_member |
| a_003 | child_1 | birth: ~1845 (derived from age) | **indirect** | indeterminate / household_member |
| a_004 | child_1 | birthplace: Ireland | direct | indeterminate / household_member |
| a_005 | child_1 | residence: Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA (1850) | direct | **primary** / enumerator witness |
| a_006 | child_1 | relationship: child_inferred of head_of_household | **indirect** | indeterminate / enumerator |
| a_007 | head_of_household | name: Thomas Flynn | direct | indeterminate / household_member |

**Key notes:**
- a_003 is indirect — birth year ~1845 is an analyst derivation (1850 − age 5), not a stated value.
- a_006 is indirect — 1850 census has no relationship column; the parent-child link is positional inference. Thomas Flynn *could* be an uncle, guardian, or other relation.
- Thomas's age/birthplace are absent from the provided GedcomX stub — a `record_read` of the household ARK would fill those in and is recommended before person-evidence on Thomas.
- No sibling stubs written: Thomas Flynn is not yet in `tree.gedcomx.json`, so the ParentChild trigger did not fire.

---

**FamilySearch record matches for MXHY-TP4 (informational only):**

| ARK | Record | Confidence | Score | Status |
|-----|--------|-----------|-------|--------|
| MABC | **1860 U.S. Federal Census** | **4** | 0.88 | pending |
| MDEF | Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1967 | 3 | 0.65 | pending |

The 1860 census match (confidence 4 / score 0.88) is high-priority — it would place Patrick at age ~15 and may name parents or siblings. The PA death certificate (confidence 3) aligns with the project's known death year of 1908 in Schuylkill County and is worth examining for parentage fields.

**Next steps:** `person-evidence` to link this record persona to I1; then `search-records` to pull the confidence-4 1860 census match (MABC) and the death certificate (MDEF).

**Next step:** Run `person-evidence` to link P1 → I1 (Patrick) and create tree stubs for Thomas (P2) and Mary (P3); then `check-warnings`.

The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All extracted assertions are factually correct. The skill accurately extracted names, ages, birthplace, residence, and inferred relationships from the 1850 census record. The log_entry_id correctly references log_001. All record_persona_ids match the GedcomX person IDs (P1, P2, P3). Evidence types are correctly assigned: direct for explicit facts (name, age, birthplace, residence), indirect for inferred relationships per pre-1880 census rules. Record IDs are complete URLs as required. No fabricated material or unsupported claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all available assertions from the provided record. All three personas (Patrick, Thomas, Mary) have their facts extracted: names, residence, Patrick's birth year and birthplace, and all relationship inferences. Both ParentChild relationships were decomposed into separate assertions (one for each parent as child_inferred, mirrored by parent_inferred assertions). The skill correctly reused log_001 as instructed rather than creating a new log entry.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Both tool calls matched live fixtures with no errors. tree_edit call correctly added the source with title, author, and URL extracted from the record. research_append call correctly appended one source entry and 13 assertion entries in atomic batch. All record_persona_ids, record_ids, and log_entry_ids are properly formed. The citation in the source entry is detailed and accurate. All assertions carry required fields including informant, informant_proximity, evidence_type, and log_entry_id.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Patrick's data decomposed into separate assertions: one for name, one for age, one for birth year (with date_certainty: estimated), one for birthplace, one for residence. The two parent relationships are separate assertions with different structured_values (child_inferred for P1, parent_inferred for P2/P3). No compound facts are crammed into single values; downstream skills can query individual facts independently.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly identified with appropriate proximity. For Patrick's name/age/birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with 'household_member' proximity, with reasoning that a 5-year-old could not self-report. For residence: 'census enumerator' with 'witness' proximity. For Thomas and Mary (household head/wife): 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with 'household_member' proximity. All informant_bias_notes explain the reasoning clearly. The skill correctly distinguished the recorder (enumerator) from the actual informant (household member) for name/age facts.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned per 1850 census rules. Direct evidence: names, ages, birthplace (explicitly stated in record), residence (directly observed). Indirect evidence: birth year (calculated from age minus census year, not explicitly stated), parent-child relationships (inferred from household position, as pre-1880 census has no relationship column). The skill correctly notes in informant_bias_notes that the ParentChild edges are 'indexer inference from household position.' All classifications align with the source document's actual content constraints.
